segments.s524-g73: This is two coordinated nominal-predicate clauses compressed for poetic meter. "رکیبش گران شد" = literally "his rākīb (with -ش = his) became heavy" (simple past 'شد' + adjective). The second clause "سبک شد عنان" inverts normal subject–verb order (poetic inversion): "عنـان" (the reins) is the subject and "سبک شد" is the predicate 'became light/slack'.
segments.s524-g74: "به گردن برآورد رخشان سنان" is a simple past clause in which the locative/prepositional phrase "به گردن" ('onto/to the neck') precedes the verb. "برآورد" is the past of برداشتن/برآوردن (here 'lifted/put/ran up'); "رخشان سنان" is the object (a noun phrase: 'shining spear') placed after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s524-g75: "پس‌اندر" is a temporal/adverbial prefix meaning 'afterwards' or 'thereupon'. "چو" introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by the nominal phrase "باد دمان اردوان" ('the wind (باد) of/at the hem/skirt (دمان) of Ardavān')—juxtaposition without an explicit ezāfe is common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s524-g76: "همی" is a continuity/emphatic particle (here marking ongoing action), followed by the past verb "تاخت" ('galloped/rushed'). The prepositional phrase "با رنج و تیره‌روان" ('with hardship and gloom/ill‑fate') accompanies the verb; the two nouns are coordinated by "و".
segments.s524-g77: "بدانگه که" is a temporal connective ('at that moment when'); "بگذشت" is the past verb 'passed'; the time-quantity "نیمی ز روز" ('half of the day') functions as the subject/time phrase and follows the verb here for poetic word order.
segments.s524-g78: This clause uses the object marker "را" to mark "فلک" ('the sky/heavens') as the object of the past verb "بپیمود" ('traversed/spanned'); the substantive epithet "گیتی فروز" ('world‑brightener' / 'that which lights the world') serves as the subject and is placed after the verb for emphasis and meter.
segments.s524-g79: "یکی" here is the indefinite/article 'a certain' modifying "شارستان" ('town/city'); the simple past verb "دید" ('saw') follows. The prepositional phrase "با رنگ و بوی" ('with color and scent') describes the town—"رنگ و بوی" is a fixed descriptive pair.
segments.s524-g80: "بسی" is an old/poetic quantifier 'many' placed before "مردم" ('people'); the past verb "آمد" ('came') follows, and the prepositional phrase "به نزدیک اوی" ('to his nearness / near him') indicates direction/goal—"اوی" is the archaic/pronounced form meaning 'him/her/it'.
segments.s524-g33: This clause is a reporting clause: یکی (one/a person) is the subject, گفت is the past-tense verb 'said', and زیشان ('of them/among them') identifies whose voice it was — together giving 'one of them said'. The conjunction که introduces the subordinate clause 'اندر گذشت' ('as/when [they] passed by'); اندر is a locative preposition 'in/within' and گذشت is the past tense 'passed'.
segments.s524-g34: دو تن is the subject 'two persons'; بر دو باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'on two occasions / a second time' (بر + باره), and درآمد is the past-formation meaning 'entered/came in'. The phrase به دشت is the goal 'into/onto the plain'. Word order is subject — adverbial — verb — goal.
segments.s524-g35: همی is a continuous/aspectual particle here ('kept on / were continually'), attached to the past plural verb برگذشتند 'they passed by'. پویان is the noun/epithet 'the moving/wayfaring ones' (the travelers) and به راه is a locative phrase 'on the road'. The clause literally reads 'the travelers kept passing on the road.'
segments.s524-g36: This is a verbless descriptor of the two persons introduced by یکی ... و دیگر: یکی introduces the first of the two and is followed by modifiers, then و دیگر introduces the second. باره here functions as an adverbial/occasional qualifier tied to the description (classical usage), and خنگ and سیاه are adjectival descriptions ('dazed/stupid' and 'black/dark'). The phrase therefore describes their appearances: 'one dazed, the other black.'
segments.s524-g37: به دم here means 'at the moment/edge/side of' or 'at the flank of' and introduces the circumstantial phrase 'at the riders' (سواران). یکی is 'one (of them)', followed by غرم پاک: غرم is a noun 'wrath/anger' and پاک 'pure/entire' modifies it — together conveying 'one wholly furious' or 'one in pure fury'. The whole phrase sets the scene 'at the riders' side, one [was] in pure anger.'
segments.s524-g38: چو is the simile 'like/as', اسپی is 'a steed/horse', and همی marks continued action. بر پراگند خاک is a poetic verbal/adverbial construction meaning '(he) scattered dust' (پراگند 'scattered', خاک 'dust') with بر indicating the action upon the dust. Together: 'like a steed he kept scattering dust.'
segments.s524-g39: به دستور ('by order' or 'on command') is an agentive/instrumental prepositional phrase (به here = 'by'); گفت is the past verb 'said', and آن زمان اردوان places the speaker and time ('Ardavan at that time'). Word order is slightly marked but yields 'Ardavan then said, by order.'
segments.s524-g40: This is the content clause introduced by که 'that/asking': این غرم باری is the noun phrase 'this uproar/burst of anger' (غرم 'uproar/anger', باری used as 'a burst/instance'), چرا is the interrogative 'why', شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/happened', and دوان (from دواندن) conveys the idea of running/rushing — here idiomatically 'arose/suddenly broke out'. Together it forms the question 'why has this uproar suddenly arisen?'
segments.s524-g57: بباید is an impersonal modal meaning ‘one must’ or ‘it is necessary’; it governs an following verb phrase. بدین چشمه is the destination: بدین = به + (این) 'to this' (poetic spelling) and چشمه 'spring/spring of water'. آمد فرود is a compound verbal idea meaning 'to come down / alight' (آمد used with فرود to express descent). The whole segment therefore means 'it is necessary to descend to this spring.'
segments.s524-g58: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for/because'. شد is simple past 'became / happened'. باره here means 'baggage / load' (i.e. what they carried). و links clauses. مرد is 'the man' and بی‌تار و پود is a fixed descriptive phrase literally 'without warp and weft' (i.e. stripped of cloth / destitute). Together: 'for the baggage was (taken) and the man was left without warp and weft.'
segments.s524-g59: بباشیم is 1st-person-plural optative/subjunctive 'let us be / let us stay' (a polite hortative). بر آب gives the location 'by/at the water' (بر = upon/at). و connects actions. چیزِی 'something' is the object of خوریم, and خوریم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative of خوردن 'let us eat / we should eat.' The clause is a hortatory invitation: 'let us stay at the water and eat something.'
segments.s524-g60: ازان پس is a time phrase 'after that / thereafter' (از آن = 'from that', پس = 'then/after'). بر آسودگی بگذریم uses بر + noun to mark the direction/goal ('toward/into rest'), and بگذریم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative of گذشتن 'let us pass / let us go on'. The clause means 'then afterward let us go on to repose/rest.'
segments.s524-g61: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. هر دو = 'both' ('هر' A = 'each/every' combined with دو yields 'both'). رسیدند is past tense 'they reached/arrived'. نزدیک آب is an adjunct of place 'near the water' (نزدیک = near, آب = water). Word order is conjunction + subject phrase + verb + locative.
segments.s524-g62: به here introduces a predicative state 'with / in (the quality of)'. زردی = 'yellowness' (a quality of complexion). دو رخساره 'two faces/complexions' specifies whose yellowness it is. چون introduces a simile 'like', and آفتاب is the simile's tenor 'the sun'. The phrase describes their faces as yellow like the sun.
segments.s524-g63: همی خواست is a past progressive/intentional construction 'was about to / intended'. کاید here functions as an infinitival/complementive element meaning 'to attempt / to set about' (poetic/archaic usage), and فرود 'descent / alighting' names the action. اردشیر (the name) is the agent; word order is inverted for poetic effect (verb before subject). Overall: 'Ardashir was about to descend/alight.'
segments.s524-g64: دو مرد جوان is the direct object 'two young men' (or the people seen). دید is past 'saw' (the subject is Ardashir carried over from the previous clause). بر آبگیر is a locative phrase 'at the pond' (بر = at/on, آبگیر = pond/reservoir). The clause thus means 'he saw two young men at the pond.'
segments.s524-g17: ندارد + نیاید forms an impersonal judgment: ندارد (lit. 'does not have') here conveys 'it is not fitting/it is not proper' and is followed by a subjunctive negative نیاید ('should not come'). The phrase به بالین من ('to my bedside') uses the preposition به + noun (بالین 'pillow/bedside') with من as possessor ('my'). Word order is verb/impersonal predicate + subordinate verb + prepositional phrase.
segments.s524-g18: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; داند is the present (poetic) 3rd-person singular of دانستن 'to know'. بدین is the preposition-like combination ب + این meaning 'by/with this' and links to داستان ('this tale'). دین من means 'my faith/religion' where من is the possessor. The whole clause modifies the implied person in the previous segment.
segments.s524-g19: بیامد is the simple past (poetic/older form) 'came'; هم‌انگاه is an adverb 'immediately'. The following two nouns مهتر دبیر form a title 'the chief scribe/secretary' (noun + noun). Typical Persian/poetic order puts the verb before its nominal subject.
segments.s524-g20: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause. رفتست is an archaic/poetic perfective form meaning 'has gone'/'went (and is gone)'. بیگاه دوش is a temporal phrase: بیگاه = 'at dawn' and دوش = 'last night' (classical usage combines to mean 'at dawn (of last night/early morning)'). اردشیر is the person named.
segments.s524-g21: This clause lists what was taken from the stable. وز is an older/poetic form equivalent to 'از' ('from') or a compound conjunction (here indicating 'and/from'); آخور is 'stable/manger'. ببردست functions adverbially 'by hand/with the hand' or 'he brought by hand'. خنگ و سیاه are two items (here proper names, likely horses) coordinated by و. Word order places the source and the taken items after the verb implied in the larger clause.
segments.s524-g22: که begins a relative clause modifying the preceding items. بد باره is a fixed compound meaning 'ill-omened' or 'inopportune' (بد + باره 'occasion/fortune'). نامبردار here means 'the one named / the aforesaid' (i.e. 'so-named'), and شاه is 'the king' — together giving 'which, by ill fortune, were named by the king' or 'which, unfortunately, had been so-called by the king.'
segments.s524-g23: هم‌انگاه 'immediately' is fronted; شد is the past of شدن 'became'; شاه را uses the topical/object marker را to mark 'the king' as the affected person; دلپذیر is the predicate adjective 'pleased/pleasant'. The construction X را Y شدن = 'X became Y' is standard.
segments.s524-g24: که introduces the reason/clause. گنجور here denotes the treasury or treasure-house (or the treasure-holder) and with او ('his') indicates possession ('his treasury' or 'his treasurer'). رفت is past 'went' and با + اردشیر means 'with Ardashir'. The clause explains why the king became pleased.
segments.s524-g9: بدربر is a fronted locative/archaic phrase marking place ('at the gate/threshold') and is placed before the clause for poetic emphasis. سپاه ایستاده is subject + participial/predicative form: سپاه (army) + ایستاده (standing/has stood) — ایستاده is the past participle used predicatively to mean 'was standing'. به پای is a prepositional phrase 'at the foot/base of' that links to the following nouns.
segments.s524-g10: بیاراسته is an adjectival participle 'unadorned/unarranged' that modifies the following coordinated nouns. تخت و تاج و سرای are three conjoined nouns (throne, crown, and palace/house), with the adjective placed before them — a common poetic inversion in Persian.
segments.s524-g11: ز درگاه is a fronted prepositional phrase 'from the court/threshold'. برخاست is the past tense verb 'rose/stepped forth'. سالار بار is a nominal phrase 'the commander/chief of the baggage' (سالار = commander; بار = load/cargo) placed after the verb — classical Persian allows verb before subject for emphasis.
segments.s524-g12: بیامد is the past tense 'came'. بر here is the preposition 'to/upon' used with آمدن to mean 'approached' or 'came before'. نامور شهریار is a noun phrase 'the renowned king' with the adjective نامور preceding شهریار.
segments.s524-g13: بدو گفت is the inverted order of گفت به او ('(they) said to him') — بدو is the dative 'to him' and گفت is the verb 'said'. گردنکشان is the agent 'the rebels/proud ones' and introduces the reported speech. بر درند combines the preposition بر + the present plural درند ('they rend/tear') used here as a gnomic/habitual statement describing the rebels' action.
segments.s524-g14: هر آنکس is an indefinite relative 'whoever/anyone'. کجا is a locative 'where/anywhere'. مهتر is 'the greater/chief' and کشورند is a predicative verb phrase 'are (of the) country/realm' — the clause says in effect 'whoever, wherever, is the chief in the land'. Note classical/poetic subject–verb agreement may look irregular (کشورند has a plural ending but refers to an indefinite/collective subject).
segments.s524-g15: پرستندگان را marks the definite/direct object with را ('to the worshippers' or 'the worshippers (as the audience)'). چنین گفت is the adverb + verb 'thus said', and شاه at the end is the subject 'the king' — placing the subject at the end is a common poetic ordering for emphasis.
segments.s524-g16: که introduces the reported clause ('that'). گلنار is here a proper name (a woman) used as the topic. چون is a comparative/linking particle 'as/like' or 'in the manner that', راه و آیین is a coordinated noun phrase 'way and custom/manner', and نگاه ('gaze/look') names the characteristic being described; together they present a poetic description of Golnār's bearing and glance. Grammatically, the adjective-like phrase (راه و آیین) is linked to نگاه to indicate manner.
segments.s524-g97: Imperative clause. یکی ('a certain/one') qualifies نامه ('letter'); بنویس is the 2nd-person imperative 'write!'. نزد + noun here marks the addressee/location 'at/to the son/young man' (word order is verb after object phrase, a normal Persian imperative sequence).
segments.s524-g98: Command continuation. به نامه introduces the medium/recipient 'in/with the letter' (lit. 'to the letter'); بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive form of 'to say' used here to mean 'say/tell'; the following این سخن is the content 'this message/these words'; در به در is an idiomatic compound (در + به + در) meaning 'door-to-door, homeless/adrift' — the two در's are 'door' and the intervening به is the preposition 'to'.
segments.s524-g99: Conditional/negative preface. نشانی ('a sign/trace/address') is the object; مگر here means 'unless'; یابد is the 3rd-person present-subjunctive/optative of یافتن 'to find' (used to express possibility/conditional: 'if he should find / unless he finds'); از + proper name indicates source 'from Ardashir.'
segments.s524-g100: Result clause introduced by نباید 'it must not (be the case)'. که is the complementizer 'that'; او 'he' is the subject; شد is the past of شدن 'became/was made' (here 'come to be, be undone/ruined'); از introduces an ablative/reason 'by/from'; غرم شیر is a genitive construction 'the lion's wrath' (غرم = wrath/anger, شیر = lion). The whole means 'it must not turn out that he is ruined by the lion's fury.'
segments.s524-g101: Temporal clause. چو 'when' + بشنید (past tense 'heard') gives 'when he heard'; ز/زو is the preposition 'from' (poetic form) and attaches to the following noun; اردوان is the proper name 'Ardavan' (here the subject who heard); این سخن 'this word/this news' is what was heard. Word order is flexible/poetic: the prepositional phrase 'from him' appears between verb and subject.
segments.s524-g102: Main clause: بدانست is the past tense 'he understood/he perceived'; کآواز is a fused relative/nominal (ک + آواز) meaning 'that the voice/report' (ک = 'that'); او 'his/him' refers back to the person in question; شد کهن is 'became old/aged' (شد = became, کهن = old/ancient). Together: 'he perceived that the report/rumor about him had grown old.'
segments.s524-g103: Locative-motion clause in poetic word order. بدان = ب + دان here functions as 'to that' (بدان) or 'into that' (poetic contraction); شارستان 'city/town' is the place; اندر آمد is the compound verb 'entered/ came into'; فرود in this position reads as the name 'Farud' placed at the end for emphasis (in epic Persian proper names often appear after the verb), so the clause means 'Farud entered that town.'
segments.s524-g104: Descriptive sentence with habitual imperfective nuance: همی (an older continuous/iterative particle) + داد (gave) yields 'kept giving / repeatedly gave'; نیکی 'kindness/good deeds' is the object; دهش را is an object/beneficiary phrase 'to the giver' or 'to the gift' (دهش = gift/that which is given) marked by را; درود = 'greeting/blessing'. Together the clause says he constantly showed kindness and sent greetings (to the giver).
segments.s524-g49: This is a subject/phrase cluster: 'جوان' (the young man) + 'با کنیزک' (with the little maid) followed by a simile introduced by 'چو' ('like/as'). 'باد دمان' is a poetic compound — literally 'wind of the hem' (a quick breeze that lifts a skirt) used as a metaphor for swiftness. Word order places the simile directly after the noun phrase.
segments.s524-g50: 'نپردخت' is a past, 3rd-person singular negative form meaning 'did not desist/cease' (classical usage: 'نپرداختن از' = 'not stop from'). It governs the verbal-nominal complement 'از تاختن' ('from galloping'), and the phrase ends with 'یک زمان' ('one moment' = 'a single moment'), specifying duration. The patient/subject (the youth and maid) is understood from the previous segment.
segments.s524-g51: 'کرا' is an archaic relative/pronominal 'whoever'. 'یار' here means a companion/ally (an associate in battle). 'باشد' functions as the copula/present-tense marker ('is' / 'has as [a friend] being'), and 'سپهر بلند' is a poetic nominative phrase 'high/lofty heaven' used metaphorically to mean exalted or high-ranking; together the phrase says 'whoever has a lofty ally.'
segments.s524-g52: 'بروبر' is an adverb meaning 'directly' or 'face-to-face' (i.e., in straight confrontation). 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from.' 'دشمن' is 'the enemy.' 'نیاید' is a negative present/optative third-person form meaning 'does not come' / 'will not come.' 'گزند' is the noun 'harm.' Together: 'from the enemy no harm will come (in a direct encounter).'
segments.s524-g53: 'ازان' ('from that') refers back to the preceding event. 'تاختن' is a verbal noun/gerrund 'the galloping/charging.' 'رنجه شد' means 'became distressed/suffered' (رنجه as predicate + شد 'became'). Word order places the verbal predicate before the proper name 'اردشیر' (subject), a common poetic inversion: 'Ardashir became distressed because of that galloping.'
segments.s524-g54: 'بدید' is the simple past 'he saw.' 'از بلندی' literally 'from on high/from a height' (indicating vantage point). 'یکی' here is the indefinite 'a certain' (not the numeral sense), and 'آبگیر' is 'a pond' or small reservoir. The sequence gives the observation: 'from a height he saw a certain pond.'
segments.s524-g55: 'جوانمرد پویان' is the subject phrase: 'جوانمرد' (a chivalrous/manly person) qualifying 'پویان' (here taken as the name/title). 'به گلنار گفت' is the reporting construction 'said to Golnār' ('به' marks the indirect object). The verb 'گفت' is the past 'said'; word order is straightforward S + IO + V.
segments.s524-g56: This is the reported content introduced by the complementizer 'که' ('that'). 'اکنون که' is a fixed idiom 'now that' (here the second 'که' functions causally: 'since/now that'). 'با رنج' ('with hardship') marks the means/by which the change occurred. 'گشتیم' is the past 1st-person plural 'we became' and 'جفت' is the predicate 'a pair' or 'mates'—so: 'that now, since by hardship we have become a pair...' (the clause likely continues in the following line).
segments.s524-g81: چنین گفت is an adverb (چنین, 'thus') placed before the verb گفت ('said'); با introduces the audience/recipient ('to' in this context) rather than accompaniment, so با موبدان نامدار = 'to the renowned mobeds'. Note the adjective نامدار follows the noun موبدان (postpositive adjective), a common pattern in Persian poetic style.
segments.s524-g82: که introduces a subordinate/quoted question clause and is followed by the interrogative کی ('who' here). The verb برگذشت is a past (preterite) third-person form 'passed by'; آن دلاور سوار is the definite noun phrase 'that valiant rider' where آن is the determiner and دلاور (adjective/noun) and سوار (head noun) follow the verb — classical Persian allows verb before subject/NP.
segments.s524-g83: چنین داد پاسخ = 'thus gave (a) reply' where چنین (thus) modifies the verb داد ('gave'); پاسخ is the direct object. بدو is the indirect-object clitic phrase 'to him' (دو = to him), and رهنمای is the agent 'the guide' placed after the object here (inversion for poetic/literary effect).
segments.s524-g84: که‌ای is an exclamatory/vocative particle (roughly 'O') introducing direct address; شاه is the vocative noun 'king' followed by epithets. نیک‌اختر here functions as an epithet/proper epithet attached to the king, and پاک‌رای is another epithet joined by و ('and'). Epithets in Persian poetical style commonly follow the noun.
segments.s524-g85: بدانگه که is a temporal phrase 'at that moment/thereupon when'; که introduces the subordinate time clause. خورشید is the subject 'the sun', برگشت is a past verb 'turned/returned' used here to mean 'became/turned', and زرد is a predicate adjective 'pale/yellow' placed after the verb as the predicative complement.
segments.s524-g86: بگسترد is a classical-form past tense verb 'spread' (note the prefixal b- in older Persian forms); it precedes its subject شب ('night') in this poetic word order. چادر لاژورد is the object 'lapis (blue) tent' where لاژورد (lapis/azure) follows and qualifies چادر.
segments.s524-g87: بدین شهر is a locative phrase 'through/in this city' (بدین = 'in/through this'); note the dataset's internal token split is irregular (ب + دین) but the function is locative. بگذشت is the past verb 'passed by/went past' and precedes the subject phrase پویان دو تن ('two active/quick persons' — پویان qualifying دو تن). The order is verb + locative + subject, typical of elevated/epic Persian.
segments.s524-g88: پر از گرد و بی‌آب گشته دهن is a sequence of modifiers describing دهن ('mouth'). پر از X = 'full of X' (پر 'full' + از 'of'); گرد here is 'dust', و links the coordinated phrase, بی‌آب 'without water/parched', and گشته is a perfect/participial form 'having become' (qualifying دهن). The whole is a postpositive adjectival/participial phrase modifying دهن.
segments.s524-g1: چنان بد که is a correlative result clause meaning “so ... that ...”; چنان is an adverb modifying بد (‘so/so much’). بی‌ماه روی is a compound adjective + noun (بی‌ماه = ‘without the moon, moonless’) placed before روی (‘face’). اردوان is the proper name in apposition to that description. Word order is typical of classical Persian poetic compression: degree → adjective → relative particle → noun phrase.
segments.s524-g2: نبودی is the past negative (here ‘was not’ / ‘would not be’ in the narrative past). It links as the main verb of the result clause introduced by که. شب و روز (= “night and day”) is an adverbial phrase modifying روشن‌روان, which is a predicate adjective ‘bright‑souled/cheerful’; the negative before implies “was not bright‑souled by night and day.”
segments.s524-g3: ز دیبا is an ablative/source phrase ‘from (the) brocade’ (poetic ز = از). نبرداشتی is a counterfactual past form (‘would not have taken/removed’); the negative + past form expresses a hypothetical result. دوش و یال are the items affected: دوش here means ‘shoulder/shoulder‑cover’ and یال ‘mane/crest’ (poetic clothing/ornament terms). The verb precedes its objects, a common poetic order.
segments.s524-g4: مگر introduces an exception/conditional (‘unless’). چهر گلنار is the object phrase ‘the face of Golnār’ (Golnār as a personal name). دیدی is simple past ‘you/he saw’ used here in the protasis of the conditional. به فال is a fixed collocation meaning ‘as an omen’ or ‘in divination’ (به is the prepositional marker used to form ‘as/by’ here). Together: ‘...would not have ... unless he had seen Golnār’s face as an omen.’
segments.s524-g5: چو = ‘when’ (temporal conjunction). آمدش is a past verb with the 3rd‑person enclitic -ش (‘came upon him’). هنگام برخاستن is a temporal noun phrase ‘the time of rising’ (هنگام + infinitive برخاستن used nominally). The whole segment means ‘when the time to rise came to him.’
segments.s524-g6: به دیبا = ‘with/into brocade’ (به used poetically to mark instrument or association: ‘with brocade’). سر is the direct object ‘head’. گاهش (= گاه + -ش) is ‘his/that occasion/time’ and modifies آراستن. آراستن is the infinitive ‘to adorn’ used as the verb of the clause; in narrative the full auxiliary (‘he did’) may be implied. Together: ‘he adorned his head with brocade (on that occasion).’
segments.s524-g7: کنیزک is the subject ‘young maid/handmaid’. نیامد is the simple past negative ‘did not come’. به بالین اوی is a prepositional phrase ‘to his/her bedside’ where بالین here means ‘by/bedside’ and اوی is the 3rd‑person enclitic pronoun marking possession (‘his/her’). Word order places the verb before the locative phrase, typical of poetic narrative.
segments.s524-g8: برآشفت is a past form ‘she became upset/angry’. و = ‘and’. پیچان شد is a light‑verb construction: پیچان (participial/adjectival stem ‘twisting, writhed’) + شد (‘became’), together ‘became twisting / writhed’. از introduces cause (‘from/because of’) and کین اوی = ‘her/ his hatred’ (کین = hatred/vengeance, اوی = 3rd‑person enclitic). The clause thus gives result and motive: she flew into anger and writhed because of (her) hatred toward him.
segments.s524-g41: چنین داد پاسخ is a poetic word order meaning “he gave such an answer”: چنين (thus) modifies the verb داد (gave, past). که introduces a content clause. آن فر اوست is a nominal clause: آن فر (“that farr”) is the topic and اوست is the copular construction literally “the farr is his” (فر + possessive او + copula ست).
segments.s524-g42: به شاهی و نیک‌اختری پر اوست: به introduces the domain/qualities (here “in/with”), شاهی و نیک‌اختری are the qualities he possesses (kingship and auspiciousness), پر is a predicative adjective “full (of)”, and اوست (او + ست) is the copula attaching the predicate to the person — literally “full of kingship and auspiciousness is he.”
segments.s524-g43: گر is the classical conditional 'if'. این غرم دریابد is a protasis where دریابد (here = reach/befall; classical/poetic use of رسیدن/دریافتن) expresses that the anger reaches him. او را متاز is an admonition with متاز functioning as a prohibitive/imperative sense 'do not be boastful/brag' with را marking او as the object/target of that admonition.
segments.s524-g44: که here introduces the reason/explanatory clause ('for/because'). این کار گردَد uses گردَد (classical subjunctive/future sense of گشتن) meaning 'will become' or 'turn into'. بمابر is a dative phrase 'for us' and دراز is the predicate adjective 'long' — together 'will become long for us' (i.e. the matter will be protracted for us).
segments.s524-g45: فرود آمد is a past intransitive verb phrase 'alighted/descended'. In this poetic order the verb phrase appears before the locative/subject: آن جایگه is 'that place' (location) and اردوان is the person (subject) who performed the action; classical Persian often allows such flexible ordering.
segments.s524-g46: A sequence of past actions joined by و: بخورد (here narrative past 'he ate'), برآسود 'he rested/was at ease', and آمد دوان where آمد is 'he went/came' and دوان functions adverbially 'running/rushing' — together 'ate, rested, and then rushed off.' The short verbs and conjunctions mark successive events.
segments.s524-g47: همی is an adverb indicating continuous or ongoing action ('kept on'). تاختند is the past plural 'they galloped/charged'. از پس + proper name forms a prepositional phrase 'after/in pursuit of Ardashir' (از + پس = 'after/behind').
segments.s524-g48: به پیش اندرون is a directional phrase: به 'toward/into' + پیش (here 'in the presence of / before') + اندرون 'inner quarters/inside', so 'toward the inner area before/where ... stand'. اردوان و وزیر names the people in those inner quarters (Ardavan and the vizier). The whole phrase indicates they rode toward the interior in front of Ardavan and the minister.
segments.s524-g89: یکی ... بود is an existential phrase meaning 'there was a certain/one ...'. یکی here has the indefinite sense 'a certain' (not the strict numeral), غرم is used as a noun 'anger/angry (one)' or 'an enraged man', and بود is the past copula 'was'. از پس is a compound prepositional idea 'after/behind', so از + پس + سوار together yields 'behind a horseman'. Word order is subject-predicate with the prepositional phrase following.
segments.s524-g90: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause referring back to the horseman; چون here means 'like/as', and او refers back to that person ('him'). ندیدم is 1st-person singular past negative 'I did not see' (so the speaker asserts he has never seen such a one). به + ایوان specifies location ('at/in the iwan/portico'), and نگار here names the decorated/painted quality ('painted'/'picture'), so the phrase as a whole says 'whose like I have not seen in the painted iwan.'
segments.s524-g91: چنین گفت is a fixed expression 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); با + name usually means 'to' or 'with' in direct speech contexts, so با اردوان کدخدای = 'to Ardavan, the headman' (addressing him). Note thatِ کدخدای is an apposition naming Ardavan's office.
segments.s524-g92: کز is a literary relative/conditional particle 'that/which/if', here introducing the content of what was said. ایدر = 'here'. مگر in this context functions as a rhetorical/questioning particle (≈ 'will you not?/is it not that...?'). بازگردی is the (subjunctive/optative) verb 'you return' (2nd person), and به جای = 'to your place/post'. So the clause is a direct question: 'Will you not return from here to your place?'
segments.s524-g93: This is an instruction made compact by nominal forms: سپه‌سازی = 'making/raising an army' (سپه + سازی 'making/formation of an army') and ساز جنگ آوری = 'bring/prepare the instruments/devices of war' (ساز used as 'means/devices' and آوری 'bring' as verb/noun-form). The coordination with و links the two imperatives/nominal commands.
segments.s524-g94: که introduces the reason or consequence clause: اکنون 'now', دگرگونه 'different/changed', شد 'became' (past copula/verb), and داوری 'judgment/decision'. The clause literally says 'for now judgment has become different,' giving the motive for raising an army.
segments.s524-g95: که again introduces explanation: بختش 'his fortune' with the possessive suffix -ش, پس پشت is a literary phrase meaning 'at the rear/behind' (پس + پشت together), او 'him', and برنشست is a past verb 'sat upon' or 'alighted on'. Idiomatically it means 'his fortune turned its back on him' (fortune no longer favored him).
segments.s524-g96: ازین is a literary form of 'از این' = 'from this'; تاختن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'the charge/charging'; باد here is the optative particle 'may' (classical Persian), and ماند is the past/stative 'remain' ('may ... remain'). به دست 'in hand' or 'to hand' indicates what should remain. Together this is an optative wish: 'From this charge, may something remain in hand.'
segments.s524-g25: دلِ مردِ جنگی is a noun phrase meaning the inner feeling/courage of the warrior; Persian here omits the ezafe so the three words stand side-by-side as a genitive construction. برآمد ز جای is a verbal phrase: برآمد (past) 'rose/issued forth' + ز (poetic 'from') + جای 'place' — literally 'his heart rose from its place.'
segments.s524-g26: Compound verb sequence: برآشفت (past) 'became agitated/was roused' coordinated with و 'and'. زود is an adverb 'quickly/at once' modifying the following verb. اندر آمد به پای is literal 'came in/into (the) foot' — idiomatically 'came down / came forward on foot' (i.e. dismounted and approached). Note the simple past tense verbs and the adverbial ordering (زود before آمد).
segments.s524-g27: سواران جنگی is the subject 'warlike horsemen / riders.' فراوان is a quantifying adverb 'in great numbers' modifying the verb. ببرد is a past narrative verb 'carried/took (away)' with its object omitted here (a common epic compression).
segments.s524-g28: تو گفتی here functions as 'you said' (second-person past); note that the lemma senses given list 'گفتی' as a noun in the metadata, but in context it is a verb (hence if the provided senses don't match, see -1 below). همی باره is a temporal/confirming phrase 'on that occasion / at that moment' (همی intensifies, باره 'occasion'). آتش سپرد is a verb phrase where سپرد (past of سپردن) is used in the sense 'consign/commit' — 'consigned to fire', i.e. 'set on fire.'
segments.s524-g29: This is a poetic word order: بره‌بر یکی نامور is 'a certain famous porter' (یکی نامور = 'a certain renowned one') and دید 'he saw' is the past verb. جای at the end marks location ('there'/'in that place'). The phrase effectively means 'he saw, in that place, a famous porter.'
segments.s524-g30: بسی is an intensive/quantifying particle here ('many' / 'much'); اندرو ('in it' / 'within there') links back to جای (the place just mentioned), so the clause states that 'in that place' (اندرو) there were many people and quadrupeds. مردم و چارپای is a simple coordinated noun phrase 'people and beasts of burden.'
segments.s524-g31: بپرسید ز ایشان is 'he asked of them' (بپرسید = asked, زیشان = 'from/of them'). که introduces the content clause (whether/that). شبگیر هور is a noun phrase in apposition: شبگیر 'the night-guard/night-watch' qualifying the proper name هور (Hoor) — i.e., 'the night-watchman Hoor.'
segments.s524-g32: شنیدی شما is an inverted interrogative order 'did you (plural) hear' (archaic/poetic word order puts the verb before the pronoun). بانگ نعلِ ستور is the object phrase 'the sound (بانگ) of the steed's horseshoe' (نعل 'horseshoe' + ستور 'steed/horse'); note the genitive relation (ezafe) is stylistically compressed in epic lines.
segments.s524-g65: جوانان (subject) + به آواز (adverbial phrase) + گفتند (past plural speech-verb). Word order is subject + adverbial + verb, typical of poetic prose; به آواز means 'with voice' = 'aloud'. زود here is what they shouted (an interjection/adverb 'quick(ly)!'), so گفتند governs the quoted/exclaimed زود. Note گفتند is the speech verb; the dictionary senses given are for a noun, so the appropriate verbal sense is not listed.
segments.s524-g66: عنان و رکیبت is a coordinated direct-object phrase: 'the reins and your rakīb'. The -ت on رکیب is the old second-person possessive clitic ('your'). بباید is the modal 'must/ought' (archaic preposed form), and بسود is an adverbial/old prepositional-type element meaning 'toward/for the (benefit/direction of)'. The clause thus gives a command: turn the reins and your mount toward (it).
segments.s524-g67: چو here = 'when/since' introducing a subordinate clause. رستی is the perfect/past sense 'you are saved / you have escaped' (second-person form of رستن). ز governs the following coordinated objects کام و دم اژدها = 'from the dragon's desire/appetite and breath' (two things one is delivered from). Word order: verb رستی precedes the prepositional phrase 'ز ...', typical poetic ordering.
segments.s524-g68: کنون 'now' (temporal adverb). آب خوردن is a verbal-noun construction 'drinking water' / 'to drink water' (آب + خوردن). نیارد is an older/poetic negative/negating verb-form meaning roughly 'will not bring/obtain' (the supplied senses do not match the verb here). بها 'price/value' is the object of نیارد; together = 'now drinking water will bring no value' → 'will be worthless / of no use.'
segments.s524-g69: نباید که is the negative impersonal/modal 'one must not / you must not'; که introduces the subordinate clause. آیی is the second-person present/subjunctive form of آمدن 'you come', here part of the idiom آیی به X فرود meaning 'to come to (fall into) X'. به خوردن فرود literally 'fall into eating' = 'to end up being eaten.' So the clause warns against descending into being eaten.
segments.s524-g70: تن خویش را = 'your body' (تن = body; خویش here corresponds to 'one's own / your own' but is split into خوی + ش in the word list; را is the direct-object marker). داد باید درود is an archaic/poetic ordering for درود باید داد = 'one must give a greeting'; داد is the verb 'give' placed before the modal باید (poetic inversion). The whole second clause therefore says 'do not (surrender) your body; one should give (only) a greeting.'
segments.s524-g71: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). از پندگوی آن = 'from that adviser' (prepositional phrase: از = from, پندگوی = 'admonisher/adviser', آن = 'that'). شنید is the past tense 'heard'. In this line the subject name اردشیر follows the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s524-g72: به گلنار گفت = 'he said to Golnār' (به marks the indirect object). این سخن = 'these/this words/this speech' (the thing said). یادگیر here is an imperative/hortative 'learn / remember' (in the word-list it's given as noun senses, but in context it's a verbal command: 'learn this saying').

sentences.s524-s37: His steed grew heavy, the reins grew slack; he lifted the shining spear to the neck.
sentences.s524-s38: Afterwards Ardavān, like the wind at his hem, still galloped on with hardship and a gloomy spirit.
sentences.s524-s39: At that moment, when half the day had passed, the world‑brightener traversed the sky.
sentences.s524-s40: He saw a certain town, with color and scent; many people came near him.
sentences.s524-s17: One of them said that, as they passed by, two people entered the plain a second time.
sentences.s524-s18: The wayfarers kept passing along the road: one (of them) dazed, the other black.
sentences.s524-s19: At the riders' flank one, all aflame with fury, like a steed kept scattering dust.
sentences.s524-s20: By command Ardavan then said: 'Why has this uproar suddenly arisen?'
sentences.s524-s29: One must come down to this spring, for the baggage was taken and the man was left without warp or weft.
sentences.s524-s30: Let us remain by the water and eat something; then afterward we will go on to rest.
sentences.s524-s31: When the two reached near the water, their two complexions were yellow like the sun.
sentences.s524-s32: Ardashir was about to alight when he saw two young men at the pond.
sentences.s524-s9: It is not fitting that he should come to my bedside who learns my faith from this tale.
sentences.s524-s10: Immediately the chief scribe arrived, for Ardashir had gone at dawn the previous night.
sentences.s524-s11: And from the stable he took (by hand) Kheng and Siyah, which, ill-omened, had been named by the king.
sentences.s524-s12: At once the king became pleased, because his treasury (or treasurer) went with Ardashir.
sentences.s524-s5: At the gate the army stood at the foot of the unadorned throne, crown, and palace.
sentences.s524-s6: From the court rose the commander of the baggage; he came up before the famed king.
sentences.s524-s7: To him the rebels said, 'They rend — whoever anywhere is the greater in the country.'
sentences.s524-s8: The king said thus to the worshippers: 'Golnār, in her bearing and her look, is (as) a manner and a way.'
sentences.s524-s49: Write a letter to a certain young man; in the letter say this message: 'he wanders from door to door.'
sentences.s524-s50: Unless he can find a trace from Ardashir, he must not be allowed to be undone by the lion's wrath.
sentences.s524-s51: When Ardavan heard this from him, he realized that the report about him had grown old.
sentences.s524-s52: Farud entered that town; he repeatedly offered kind words and sent greetings to the giver.
sentences.s524-s25: The young man with the little maid, like a breeze at the hem, did not cease galloping for a single moment.
sentences.s524-s26: Whoever has an ally of lofty stature — in a direct face-to-face encounter no harm will come from the enemy.
sentences.s524-s27: Ardashir was troubled by that galloping; from on high he saw a certain pond.
sentences.s524-s28: The brave Poyān said to Golnār that now, since by hardship we have become a pair...
sentences.s524-s41: He said thus to the renowned mobeds, 'Who was that valiant rider who passed by?'.
sentences.s524-s42: Thus the guide gave him this reply: 'O king, Nīk-akhtar and pure-minded one.'
sentences.s524-s43: Then, when the sun had turned pale, night spread its lapis-blue tent.
sentences.s524-s44: Through that city passed two travelers — two persons, their mouths full of dust and parched.
sentences.s524-s1: So gloomy that Ardavan's face was moonless, and day and night he was not bright‑souled.
sentences.s524-s2: He would not have removed his cloak and crest of brocade last night, had he not seen Golnār's face as an omen.
sentences.s524-s3: When the time to rise came upon him, he adorned his head with brocade.
sentences.s524-s4: The young maid did not come to his bedside; she became angry and writhed / twisted in hatred toward him.
sentences.s524-s21: He replied thus: that the farr (divine royal glory) is his — he is full of kingship and auspiciousness.
sentences.s524-s22: If this anger befalls him, do not boast about him; for this affair will become protracted for us.
sentences.s524-s23: Ardavan alighted at that place, ate and rested, and then dashed off.
sentences.s524-s24: They continued to gallop after Ardashir, rushing toward the inner quarters before Ardavan and the vizier.
sentences.s524-s45: There was a certain angry man behind a horseman, one whose like I had never seen in the painted iwan.
sentences.s524-s46: Thus he said to Ardavan, the headman: 'Will you not return from here to your place?'
sentences.s524-s47: Raise an army and bring the implements of war, for now judgment has turned different.
sentences.s524-s48: For his fortune turned its back on him; from this charge, may something remain in hand.
sentences.s524-s13: The warrior's heart leapt from its place; he was stirred and quickly came down on foot.
sentences.s524-s14: Many warlike horsemen carried them off in great numbers; you spoke, and at that moment he consigned the load to the fire.
sentences.s524-s15: He saw there a certain famous porter; in that place were many people and beasts of burden.
sentences.s524-s16: He asked them, 'O night-guard Hoor, did you hear the sound of the steed's horseshoe?'
sentences.s524-s33: The young men cried aloud, "Quick!" — you must turn the reins and your mount toward it.
sentences.s524-s34: Since you have escaped the dragon's appetite and breath, now drinking water will be of no value.
sentences.s524-s35: You must not come down to be eaten; do not give up your body — you should give (only) a greeting.
sentences.s524-s36: When Ardashir heard that from the adviser, he said to Golnār, 'Learn these words.'
